ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for fishball noodles (dry). The closest defensible FoodOn class is 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595), used as a closest_parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish — composite mixed dish with noodles as primary ingredient
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (composite noodle-based dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for this Singaporean hawker noodle dish. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for fishball noodles.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular hawker food entries.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of fishball noodles (dry) is approximately 300 g, comprising a serving of noodles (~180 g), three to five fish balls (~80 g), sliced lettuce (~30 g), and sauce (~10 g).

nutrient_source.title: Fishball Noodles (Dry) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fishball Noodles (Dry), cr_id F05060056, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — noodle dishes analogous estimate; GI of yellow wheat noodles (boiled) approximately 58
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean fishball noodles (dry) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: boiled yellow wheat noodles / mee pok (GI ~58, medium), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 45.24) / 100 ≈ 26.2, rounded to 26.1 using 300 g serving × 15.08 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 45.24 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 397 mg per 100 g; a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,191 mg sodium, representing 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and chili sauce, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to reduce sodium load from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 58 combined with a high glycemic load of approximately 26 per standard serving (300 g, ~45 g carbohydrate) from refined yellow noodles with minimal fibre modulation (0.8 g per 100 g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce noodle portion size, request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives where available, and increase vegetable content to improve fibre intake and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content at 6.73 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 2.1 g per 100 g from lard and processed fish balls, combined with high sodium at 397 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request the dish prepared without lard, choose a smaller noodle portion, and avoid extra soy-based sauces to limit combined saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed fish balls with high sodium and potential additives, refined noodles with very low fibre (0.8 g per 100 g), and absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit fish ball portions, add fresh leafy vegetables such as bok choy or spinach, and request reduced lard and soy sauce to lower sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fishball Noodles (Dry) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of flat yellow wheat noodles or mee pok tossed in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, chili sauce, and often lard, served with boiled or fried fish balls and sliced lettuce. Unlike the soup version, the dry preparation involves draining the noodles and coating them in the sauce mixture, resulting in a richer, more intensely flavoured dish. Fish balls are made from processed minced fish paste, giving them a springy, bouncy texture. The dish is a staple at hawker centres and food courts across Singapore and is commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 465 kcal, 25.7 g protein, 20.2 g fat, and 45.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at 397 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,191 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the refined wheat noodle base, with a glycemic load of approximately 26 per serving, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Dietary fibre is low at 0.8 g per 100 g, providing minimal modulation of postprandial glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to fish content, and contains gluten and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060056) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for boiled yellow wheat noodles, which is the closest defensible approved-source match.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the HPB database reports 0.0 g sugar per 100 g for this dish, consistent with a savoury sauce base without added sugar in the nutrient profile. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05060056, and no equivalent breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Singaporean preparation as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 2.1 g per 100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable noodle dishes with lard and fish content, as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB fatty acid breakdown if available. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on boiled yellow wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean fishball noodles (dry) is available in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (58 × 45.24 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 26.2, reported as 26.1. Halal status is not confirmed — fish balls may contain non-halal additives depending on the hawker stall; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific vendor.
